title: Dyeing wool with walnut hulls
subtitle: A tannin-rich, mordant-free brown dye from fresh or dried black walnut hulls
excerpt: Walnut hulls contain high concentrations of juglone, a natural dye compound that bonds to wool without a mordant. The colour ranges from tan to deep warm brown depending on hull freshness and fibre preparation. Walnut dye stains everything it touches, including skin and clothing, so protective equipment is important.
sourceNotes: Walnut hull dyeing is documented in Jenny Dean, Wild Colour (1999) and Dominique Cardon, Natural Dyes (2007). The mordant-free method described follows standard UK practice.

attrs.body: Walnut hulls stain skin and fabric permanently. Wear gloves and old clothes. Keep dye pots food-free.
attrs.title: Mordant safety

content.text: in the hulls bonds directly to wool. This makes it an accessible first dye for anyone without mordant chemicals. The main practical caution is the staining: walnut will permanently mark skin, work surfaces, and clothing. Set up on newspaper or old sheeting and wear gloves throughout.

items.name: Wool yarn, scoured and wet out
Fresh green walnut hulls, or dried hulls
Stainless steel dye pot, minimum 5 litre

content.text: Place the hulls in the dye pot with enough water to cover. Bring to a simmer and hold for 1 hour. Strain off all hull material. The liquid will be a deep brown-black. For a paler tan colour, dilute the dye bath with additional water.

Add the wet wool to the dye bath at around 50°C. Heat slowly to 80°C and hold for 45-60 minutes, turning the wool gently every 10-15 minutes. Remove, rinse in water of the same temperature, then rinse again in cooler water. Walnut colour will continue to deepen slightly as it dries.

Walnut also works as a cold dye: place the hulls in a jar of cold water and leave for 2-3 weeks to ferment and extract. Strain and use the cold liquid as a dye bath without heating. The colour range from cold extraction tends toward tan and khaki rather than the deeper browns from a hot bath.

items.fix: Rinse the wool thoroughly in three changes of water after dyeing and wash with a small amount of wool wash before drying.
items.cause: Residual hull material or fermented dye liquid not fully rinsed out
items.problem: The dyed wool smells unpleasant after drying

items.fix: Turn the wool every 10 minutes throughout dyeing. Walnut exhausts quickly and unevenness sets fast.
items.cause: The wool was not moved enough in the dye bath, allowing concentrated dye to pool on the fibre
items.problem: The brown colour is uneven with darker patches
